coredata.dc:title: Facile in-situ fabrication of ZnO2/CQD composites with promoted visible-light photocatalytic activities for organic degradation and bacterial inactivation

coredata.dc:description: Herein, the novel zinc peroxide/carbon quantum dots (ZnO2/CQD) composites were synthesized by an in-situ composite process for visible-light photocatalytic tetracycline (TC) degradation and Escherichia coli (E. coli) inactivation. It was found that 0.1 g/L ZnO2/CQD composite could degrade over 80 % of 50 mg/L TC and inactivate around 7-log E. coli cells after 2 h and 50 min of irradiation, respectively. The as-obtained ZnO2/CQD composites exhibited superior visible-light photocatalytic performance to individual ZnO2, and superoxide radical (•O2 −) and singlet oxygen (1O2) were identified to be the main reactive oxygen species (ROSs) for TC degradation and E. coli inactivation. The introduction of CQD reduced the particle size and increased specific surface area, benefiting for adsorption. Meanwhile, it promoted visible-light absorption and accelerated the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs with a 3-fold increase in photocurrent, thus producing more ROSs under visible-light irradiation. Moreover, the ZnO2/CQD composites exhibited a high adaptability towards initial solution pH, common coexisting anions, oxygen-deficientenvironment, diverse water sources and organic pollutants. This study not only provided highly adaptable visible-light-driven photocatalysts for organic degradation and bacterial inactivation, but also shed light on the multifunctional roles of CQD in boosting visible-light photocatalytic performance.

XRD patterns (a), FTIR spectra (b), N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms (c) and pore size distribution curves (d) of as-prepared samples.
High-resolution XPS spectra of Zn 2p (a), O 1 s (b), C 1 s (c) and N 1 s (d) in individual ZnO2 and ZnO2/CQD-0.5.
SEM images of ZnO2 (a) and ZnO2/CQD-0.5 (b), TEM (c) and HRTEM (d) images of ZnO2/CQD-0.5.
Photocatalytic experiments of TC degradation (a); Linear forms of pseudo-second order kinetic model (b); effects of initial pH on TC degradation via ZnO2/CQD-0.5; Removal efficiency of COD (d).
Adaptability experiments of ZnO2/CQD-0.5 towards common inorganic anions (a), natural water resource (b) and organic pollutant type (c); reusability tests of ZnO2/CQD-0.5 for TC degradation (d).
Inactivation activities of ZnO2 and ZnO2/CQD-0.5 towards E. coli (a) and fitting curves with Weibull model (b).

Quenching experiments (a), kinetic rate constants with the addition of different scavengers (b); EPR tests for 1O2 (c) and •O2 − (d).
H2O2 slow-release test (a) and the comparative photocatalytic degradation experiments (b).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the novel zinc peroxide/carbon quantum dots (ZnO2/CQD) composites were synthesized by an in-situ composite process for visible-light photocatalytic tetracycline (TC) degradation and Escherichia coli (E. coli) inactivation. It was found that 0.1 g/L ZnO2/CQD composite could degrade over 80 % of 50 mg/L TC and inactivate around 7-log E. coli cells after 2 h and 50 min of irradiation, respectively. The as-obtained ZnO2/CQD composites exhibited superior visible-light photocatalytic performance to individual ZnO2, and superoxide radical (•O2 −) and singlet oxygen (1O2) were identified to be the main reactive oxygen species (ROSs) for TC degradation and E. coli inactivation. The introduction of CQD reduced the particle size and increased specific surface area, benefiting for adsorption. Meanwhile, it promoted visible-light absorption and accelerated the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs with a 3-fold increase in photocurrent, thus producing more ROSs under visible-light irradiation. Moreover, the ZnO2/CQD composites exhibited a high adaptability towards initial solution pH, common coexisting anions, oxygen-deficientenvironment, diverse water sources and organic pollutants. This study not only provided highly adaptable visible-light-driven photocatalysts for organic degradation and bacterial inactivation, but also shed light on the multifunctional roles of CQD in boosting visible-light photocatalytic performance.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, water pollutions including industrial wastewater, agricultural wastewater, medical wastewater and domestic wastewater, had attracted an increasing concern, because they seriously threatened human health and ecological balance [1–3]. Organic antibiotics and pathogenic organisms were commonly considered as the major contaminants in wastewater, due to their low natural attenuation, high toxicities, and even inducing resistance genes [4–6]. Photocatalysis, which could produce multiple reactive species, had been extensively applied in various wastewater treatments, due to the convenience and high efficiency of the treating processes [7,8]. So far, diverse photocatalysts, like ZnO [9], TiO2 [10], g-C3N4 [11], BiOBr [12] and semiconductor composites [13,14], had been developed for antibiotic degradation and bacterial inactivation. Whereas, the production of reactive oxygen species (ROSs) with strong oxidative abilities was usually dependent on oxygen (O2) in the environment, because the generated ROSs in the photocatalytic processes mainly originated from the reduction of O2 by photogenerated electrons [15–17]. Therefore, oxygen in the system was very important for the application of photocatalytic technology in environmental remediation.

Under anaerobic and oxygen-deficient conditions, the application of conventional photocatalysts was restricted, owing to low-level ROS production [16]. Compared with other photocatalysts, zinc peroxide (ZnO2) was also an oxygen-supplying material through its slow self-decomposition (Eq. (1)) and hydrolysis (Eqs. (2) and (3)) [18,19], which would be conductive to ROS generation in oxygen-deficient or oxygen-free situation. However, ZnO2 could be only excited by ultraviolet (UV) light, due to the relatively broad band gap (3.3 ∼ 4.6 eV) [20]. Thus, the low utilization efficiency of solar energy severely limited its practical application. Therefore, it was of great significance to improve the visible-light photocatalytic activity of ZnO2.
(1) 2ZnO2 → 2ZnO + O2
(2) ZnO2 + 2H2O → H2O2 + Zn(OH)2
(3) 2H2O2 → 2H2O + O2

Carbon quantum dots (CQD), with merits of unique up-conversion photoluminescence, easy preparation, low toxicity and high affinity [21,22], had attracted increasing attention in enhancing the visible-light absorption ability of photocatalysts [23,24]. Meanwhile, CQD possessed an outstanding electron transport property, which could effectively accelerate the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, facilitating photocatalytic performance [25]. Coupling g-C3N4 with CQD exhibited 14-fold increase in the degradation efficiency for tetracycline than individual g-C3N4 [26]. The TiO2/CQD composite effectively degraded Rhodamine B at 23 times the rate constant of pure TiO2 [27]. To the best of our knowledge, there were few studies on the photocatalysis by ZnO2/CQD composites. For instance, El-Shamy modified ZnO2 with CQD via a physical mixing process to degrade Rhodamine B under UV light irradiation [28]. However, the visible-light photocatalytic performance and specific contributions of CQD to the improved photocatalytic activity had not been investigated yet.

In this work, ZnO2/CQD composites were fabricated by an in-situ composite process, and the visible-light photocatalytic performance towards tetracycline (TC) degradation and Escherichia coli (E. coli) inactivation were explored in details. The enhanced visible-light photocatalytic activity of ZnO2/CQD was confirmed by a series of comparative experiments and photoelectrochemicalmeasurements. Besides, the generated ROSs during the photocatalytic process were probed and identified by the quenching experiments and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) tests. Combined with the related characterizations and degradation experiments, the multifunctional roles of CQD were specifically discussed and depicted during the visible-light-induced TC degradation and E. coli inactivation over ZnO2/CQD.

Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O), urea (CH4N2O), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O, 25–28 wt%), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%), ethanol (C2H5OH), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 95–98 wt%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium chloride (NaCl), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), methylene blue (C16H18N3ClS, MB), malachite green (C23H25N2Cl, MG), Congo red (C32H22N6Na2O6S2, CR), furfuryl alcohol (C5H6O2, FFA), acetone (C3H6O), p-benzoquinone (C6H4O2, p-BQ) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (C10H16N2O8, EDTA) was brought from Shantou Jinsha Chemical Plant Co., ltd. Potassium dichromate (K2Cr2O7) was provided by Kermel Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. Isopropanol (C3H8O, IPA) was supported by Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. Tetracycline (C22H24N2O8), tetracycline hydrochloride (C22H25N2O8Cl, TCH), doxycycline hydrochloride (C22H25N2O8Cl, DOXH), 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP, 98 wt%) and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO, 97 wt%) were supplied by Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. TiO2 (P25) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Reagent Co., ltd. All chemicals were applied without further purification.

The CQD colloid was prepared via a modified hydrothermal method [29]. Briefly, 3 g of citric acid monohydrate and 1 g of urea were dissolved into 25 mL of deionized water (DI water) under vigorous stirring. Then, the mixture was sonicated for 0.5 h and transferred into a 50 mL of Teflon autoclave, which was then kept at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling down to ambient temperature, the obtained yellow–brown solution was filtered by a 0.22-μm filter. Ultimately, the filtrate was preserved at 4 °C in a refrigerator for further use.

Inspired by the one-pot preparation of ZnO2 reported in our previous work [30], the ZnO2/CQD composites were synthesized by a facile in-situ method. In a typical synthetic process, 2.98 g of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 20 mL of DI water, and then a certain volume of the as-obtained CQD colloid (0.05, 0.2, 0.5 and 1 mL) was introduced into the mixture under magnetic stirring. Subsequently, 1.6 mL of NH3·H2O was dropwise added into the above solution. After 20 min, 1.5 mL of H2O2 was added and the mixture was stirred for another 4 h at room temperature (25 °C). Thereafter, the generated precipitate was collected by centrifugation and washed several times. Finally, the products were obtained after being dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h. For convenience, the as-synthesized ZnO2/CQD composites with different CQD content were labeled as ZnO2/CQD-0.05, ZnO2/CQD-0.2, ZnO2/CQD-0.5 and ZnO2/CQD-1, respectively. Similarly, pristine ZnO2 was prepared through the same procedure without addition of CQD colloid.

The crystalline phases of products were investigated by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, X' Pert3 powder), and the chemical functional groups were determined by a Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Vertex 70). Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas of the as-synthesized samples were measured by a surface area analyzer (Micromeritics ASAP 2020). The morphology and microstructure of materials were observed by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Nova NanoSEM 450) and a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, Tecnai G2 F30), respectively. The valence states of chemical elements were identified by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, AXIS-ULTRA DLD-600 W). Chemical oxygen demand (COD) of the reaction solution was measured by a multi-parameter water quality detector (HM-800A). The photoluminescence (PL) properties of samples were confirmed by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-5301PC), and the optical features were explored by an UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrometer (UV-2550). The electrochemical measurements, including Mott-Schottky, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and photocurrent, were conducted in 0.5 M Na2SO4 electrolyte through an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E) equipped with a standard three-electrode system. The working electrode was obtained by loading photocatalysts on the fluorine-tin oxide (FTO) glass (2 cm × 2 cm). The counter electrode and reference electrode were platinum electrode and silver/silver chloride electrode (SSCE), respectively. More details of characterizations were provided in Supplementary Information.

The photocatalytic TC degradation activity was evaluated by dispersing 10 mg of photocatalyst into 100 mL of TC solution (50 mg/L), and a 300 W xenon lamp with a 400 nm cut-off filter was applied as a visible-light source. Before light irradiation, the mixture of photocatalyst and TC solution was stirred for 1 h in the dark to reach the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. After the lamp was turned on, 3 mL of suspension was sampled at a certain time interval and immediately filtered through a 0.22-μm filter to remove the solid residual. The concentration of remaining TC in solution was determined by an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (UV-670). The degradation efficiency of TC was calculated by the following equation (Eq. (4)). C0 and Ct (mg/L) were the concentrations of TC at the initial time and certain time t, respectively.
(4) Degradation efficiency (%) = (1- Ct/C0) × 100 %

E. coli was used to evaluate the bacterial inactivation performance of ZnO2/CQD composites under visible-light irradiation. Firstly, the bacterium was cultivated in the nutrient solution, which was continuously agitated at 37 °C for 15–20 h. Then, the bacterial cells were harvested by centrifugation and washed twice with sterilized saline (0.9 wt% NaCl) solution. Subsequently, the bacterial cells were re-suspended into the fresh sterilized saline solution, where the cell density was diluted to around 1 × 107 colony forming unit (CFU)/mL. After that, 5 mg of ZnO2/CQD composites was introduced into 50 mL of the bacterial suspension under constant stirring at ambient temperature, and the xenon lamp was turned on to start the bacterial inactivation process. At the given time intervals, an aliquot of reaction solution was collected and serially diluted with sterilized saline solution. Thereafter, 0.1 mL of the diluted sample was uniformly dispersed onto nutrient agar plates and immediately incubated at 37 °C for 20 h. Finally, the number of survival E. coli cells could be determined by counting the bacterium colonies on the plates. For comparison, the bacterial inactivation experiments under dark condition and the inactivation experiments of individual ZnO2 were also conducted at the same time.

In order to confirm the generated ROSs, EPR measurements were conducted on a Bruker EMXmicro-6/1 spectrometer using DMPO and TEMP as spin-trapping agents. Specifically, 2 mg of ZnO2/CQD composite was added into 0.5 mL of ethanol and subsequently mixed with 60 μL of DMPO. After that, the mixture was irradiated by visible-light for 15 min, and a small amount of the reaction solution was immediately introduced into a capillary for EPR analysis. Similarly, 2 mg of ZnO2/CQD composite was dispersed into 0.5 mL of acetone, followed by mixing with 60 μL of TEMP for EPR test. The measurement parameters were set as following: modulation frequency of 100 kHz, microwave frequency of 9.14 GHz, microwave power of 3 mW, constant time of 30 ms, scanning time of 30 s. As a contrast, the ZnO2 photocatalytic system was also characterized by EPR technique.

The optical properties of as-prepared CQD were explored by the UV–vis absorption spectrum and photoluminescence (PL) spectra with the excitation wavelength range of 300–900 nm. As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, there were two absorption peaks at 235 nm and 332 nm in the studied range (200–600 nm), which could be assigned to the typical π − π* and n − π* transition of conjugated C = X (X = O, N), respectively [31,32]. Clearly, the as-prepared CQD also exhibited a weak absorption in the visible-light region, which was consistent with the reported CQD [22] (Fig. S1a). Besides, the promoting visible-light absorption of CQD could be confirmed by the higher absorbance of ZnO2/COD solution in the visible-light range, in comparison with that of ZnO2 solution (Fig. S1b). The insert of Fig. 1a indicated that the CQD colloid possessed a bright green emission under UV light irradiation. Fig. 1b showed the peaks in PL spectra of CQD colloid shifted to the longer emission wavelength when the corresponding excitation wavelength varied in the range of 300–550 nm, suggesting the down-conversion feature of CQD [29]. Notably, the CQD colloid also exhibited a remarkable up-conversion feature. It could be observed from Fig. 1c that the emission spectra were mainly located between 380 and 580 nm when the excitation wavelength exceeded 600 nm. This point implied that the long-wavelength visible light could be converted into the short-wavelength UV light via the as-prepared CQD, which was in agreement with previous reports [22,29,33].

Fig. 2 a exhibited the XRD patterns of ZnO2/CQD composites, accompanied with individual ZnO2 for comparison. Apparently, the characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ = 31.8°, 36.9°, 53.3° and 63.3° in all samples were well indexed to the (111), (200), (220) and (311) planes of cubic ZnO2 with Pa-3 space group (JCPDS #76–1364). It could be observed that the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the main diffraction peak at 36.9° became broader with the increase of CQD dosage. According to Scherrer equation, the crystallite sizes of ZnO2 and ZnO2/CQD-0.5 composite were estimated to be 10.47 and 8.55 nm, respectively (Table S1). These results demonstrated that the introduction of CQD reduced the particle size of ZnO2 [34], which would cause more surface active sites available.

As shown in Fig. 2b, the ZnO2/CQD composites and pristine ZnO2 presented the similar FTIR vibration mode. The absorption peak around 1385 cm−1 was assigned to the characteristic vibration of OO bond [30], and the peak at 424 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of Zn-O bond. The peaks at 3435 and 1630 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of OH group and CO bond, respectively [35]. The absorption peak at 1483 cm−1 was associated with the vibration of carbonate species. Notably, the peak at 1385 cm−1 slightly weakened with the addition of CQD, while the peak around 1630 cm−1 gradually increased. This phenomenon was related to 1) the decrease of the relative content of ZnO2 in the composites with the increase of CQD dosage; 2) a possible coordination effect between C atom in CQD and OO bond in ZnO2. Fig. 2c and 2d displayed the nitrogen (N2) adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distribution curves of ZnO2/CQD-0.5 and ZnO2. Clearly, the specific surface area of ZnO2/CQD-0.5 was 263.6 m2/g (Table S1), far higher than that of pristine ZnO2 (147.6 m2/g), which indicated that the addition of CQD might improve the specific surface area and adsorption performance of ZnO2.

XPS technique was conducted to investigate the element composition and chemical valence of ZnO2 and ZnO2/CQD-0.5. The wide-survey XPS spectra (Fig. S2) showed that Zn, O and C were the main elements in the two samples. The high-resolution Zn 2p XPS spectra (Fig. 3 a) revealed that two peaks at 1022.1 and 1045.2 eV (assigned to Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 of Zn2+, respectively) were shifted to the lower binding energy positions after CQD modification, demonstrating an increase in electron cloud density of Zn atom. Also, the O 1 s peak of ZnO2/CQD-0.5 (Fig. 3b) moved clearly to lower binding energies, compared to pure ZnO2. Further, the O 1 s spectrum of ZnO2 could be divided into three overlapping peaks at 532.2 eV, 532.7 and 533.8 eV, which were attributed to Zn-O, OO and OH bond, respectively [36]. Whereas, in the O 1 s spectrum of ZnO2/CQD-0.5, two new subpeaks at 530.5 and 531.2 eV.

were identified to the CO and CO bond, respectively [37]. Notably, after CQD addition, the OH signal almost disappeared, and the relative intensity of OO bond decreased significantly. All the results implied that CQD might combine with the OO bond of ZnO2 in the composite, which was well in agreement with the FTIR analyses. As for C 1 s spectra (Fig. 3c), the peak at 285.0 eV in ZnO2 spectrum was attributed to CC bond of carbon contamination [35], which was used as a reference for peak calibration. Compared with the C 1 s spectrum of ZnO2, three new peaks could be observed at 285.7, 288.5 and 289.2 eV in the spectrum of ZnO2/CQD-0.5, which were attributed to CN, CO and CO bond, respectively [35,38]. In the high-resolution N 1 s XPS spectra (Fig. 3d), a weak peak related to CN bond was also recognized at 399.2 eV in the ZnO2/CQD-0.5 sample [39], while no signal was detected in the ZnO2 sample. The evidence of CN bond suggested that the as-prepared CQD were N-doped CQD.

The SEM images of ZnO2 and ZnO2/CQD-0.5 were displayed in Fig. 4 a and Fig. 4b, respectively. Clearly, the ZnO2 sample was mainly composed of numerous spheres with an average size of 116.4 nm (inset of Fig. 4a). After CQD modification, the average particle size could be significantly reduced to 75.4 nm (inset of Fig. 4b). Accordingly, these smaller spherical particles would tend to expose more active sites, which would facilitate the adsorption and photocatalytic performance. Meanwhile, the elemental mappings of ZnO2/CQD-0.5 (Fig. S3) demonstrated that Zn, O, C and N were homogeneously distributed in the composite. The ZnO2/CQD-0.5 sample was further characterized by TEM and HRTEM. As depicted in Fig. 4c, the ZnO2/CQD-0.5 particles displayed the sphere-like shape, in agreement with the SEM image. Seen from the HRTEM image (Fig. 4d), two sets of lattice fringe were homogeneously distributed in the detection region. The lattice fringe with an interplanar spacing of 0.28 nm was assigned to the (111) plane of ZnO2 [28], and the other lattice fringe with an interplanar spacing of 0.24 nm was well indexed to the (100) plane of CQD [23]. The results indicated that the as-prepared composite contained ZnO2 and CQD, indirectly confirming the successful fabrication of ZnO2/CQD composite.

To explore the effect of initial pH on TC photocatalytic degradation by ZnO2/CQD-0.5, the initial pH value of TC solution was adjusted to 2–12 using 0.1 M NaOH and 0.1 M H2SO4, respectively. The results (Fig. 5c) indicated that TC could be effectively degraded in a wide pH range of 4–12. When the initial pH was adjusted to 2, only 11.4 % of TC was degraded after 4 h of irradiation, because ZnO2 in the composite was completely decomposed by plenty of hydrogen ions (H+) in strong acid solution (Eq. (5)) [18]. As the initial pH rose to 4, more than 80 % of TC could be degraded within 3 h of irradiation. Further increasing the initial pH, the degradation efficiency continuously rose, which might be due to more ROS generation in strong alkaline solution [51]. Furthermore, the change in COD of the degradation system was monitored to evaluate the photocatalytic oxidation ability of ZnO2/CQD-0.5. As shown in Fig. 5d, after 3 h of irradiation, the COD removal rate was around 57 %. Extending the irradiation time to 6 h, over 75 % of COD could be removed, which manifested a high feasibility of ZnO2/CQD-0.5 in the photocatalytic treatment of TC-polluted wastewater.
(5) ZnO2 + 2H+→H2O2 + Zn2+

It was well known that the adaptability of photocatalysts was a critical indicator of the photocatalytic performance. Firstly, the adaptability of ZnO2/CQD-0.5 was evaluated by common inorganic anions (10 mM of Cl-, SO4 2-, NO3 – and HCO3 –, respectively). As depicted in Fig. 6 a, SO4 2- and NO3 – could rarely affect TC degradation by ZnO2/CQD-0.5, while HCO3 – could obviously improve TC degradation. It could be owing to a higher initial pH value (8.38) when HCO3 – was introduced into TC solution (Fig. S6a). Conversely, Cl- possessed a slight inhibition effect on TC degradation, probably resulting from the conversion of ROSs to the less active chlorine radicals during the photocatalytic process [52]. Secondly, various water resources (Fig. 6b) including DI water, tap water (Wuhan, China), East Lake water (E:114° 23′ 55″, N:30° 33′ 1″) and Yujia Lake water (E:114° 25′ 41″, N:30° 31′ 47″) were employed to probe the adaptability of ZnO2/CQD-0.5. Surprisingly, the photocatalytic degradation of TC was enhanced in these natural water resource, which might be attributed to the contribution of high initial pH of TC solutions prepared by natural water resources (Fig. S6b).

Further, the photocatalytic performance of ZnO2/CQD-0.5 in the oxygen- deficientenvironment was explored, and comparative degradation experiments were conducted by injecting argon (Ar) into TC solution and using TiO2 (Fig. S7). As expected, the introduction of Ar could rarely affect the TC degradation by ZnO2/CQD-0.5, which suggested a good photocatalytic activity of ZnO2/CQD-0.5 even in the oxygen-deficientenvironment, superior to conventional photocatalysts. In addition, diverse types of organic pollutants (10 mg/L MB, 50 mg/L TCH, 50 mg/L DOXH, 50 mg/L MG and 50 mg/L CR) were selected to explore the adaptability of ZnO2/CQD-0.5. As displayed in Fig. 6c, ZnO2/CQD-0.5 could effectively degrade these organics under visible-light irradiation, and the removal efficiencies could reach to 80.3, 77.5, 89.5, 87.2 and 99.7 % after 4 h, respectively. Therefore, it could be believed that ZnO2/CQD-0.5 possessed a high adaptability to coexisting inorganic anion, water resource, oxygen environment and type of organic pollutants.

Also, the reusability of ZnO2/CQD-0.5 was examined by adding the used solid residue into fresh TC solution (100 mL, 50 mg/L) after each degradation cycle. As expressed in Fig. 6d, although the degradation efficiency continuously decreased with the increase of cycle number, around 60 % of TC could be still degraded after the fourth cycle. Moreover, the collected solid residue after TC degradation was characterized by XRD and XPS (Fig. S8). Compared to the fresh sample, there existed a new weak peak at 18.0° in the XRD pattern of used ZnO2/CQD-0.5, which could be indexed to the (001) plane of hexagonal Zn(OH)2 (JCPDS #72–2032). Additionally, the Zn 2p and O 1 s XPS peaks of used ZnO2/CQD-0.5 were shifted to higher binding energy positions. These findings manifested that a small amount of ZnO2 in the ZnO2/CQD-0.5 composite decomposed into Zn(OH)2 (Eq. (2)), which might be the main reason for the decrease in photocatalytic performance of ZnO2/CQD-0.5 after several runs. Based on the above investigations, it could be concluded that ZnO2/CQD-0.5 possessed relatively high adaptability and reusability in the visible-light photocatalytic treatment of organic-polluted wastewater.

The bacterial inactivation activities of ZnO2/CQD-0.5 and individual ZnO2 were investigated under same conditions. As shown in Fig. 7 a, both of them could hardly inactivate E. coli in the dark. Only a slight decrease of bacterial density (around 0.5-log) was observed after 60 min, which might be ascribed to the physical adsorption of E. coli on photocatalysts. Under visible-light irradiation, it could be found that the bacterial density in the ZnO2/CQD-0.5 photocatalytic system dropped faster than that in the ZnO2 photocatalytic system. Around 7-log E. coli could be completely inactivated within 50 min by ZnO2/CQD-0.5, while an extended irradiation time (60 min) was required for ZnO2, suggesting that more ROSs could be produced in the ZnO2/CQD-0.5 photocatalytic system for E. coli inactivation.

Meanwhile, the E. coli inactivation kinetics of the ZnO2 and ZnO2/CQD-0.5 photocatalytic systems were explored by the Weibull model (Eq. (6)) [53]. Where, N0 and N represent the number of surviving bacteria at the initial time and certain time t, respectively. The parameter δ is the demanded time to inactivate the first Log10 cycle of the microbial population, and p is the power of the Weibull model, determining the shape of bacterial survival curve. The fitting results (Fig. 7b) indicated that the p value of ZnO2/CQD-0.5 curve was 1.7, higher than that of ZnO2 curve (1.5), which meant that the number of survival bacterial cells fell more quickly in the ZnO2/CQD-0.5 photocatalytic system. Thus, the bacterial inactivation experiments also proved that the visible-light photocatalytic activity of ZnO2 could be improved by CQD modification, in consistency with the results of above photocatalytic degradation experiments.
(6)
log
N
N 0
= -
t δ
p

UV–vis DRS characterizations were carried out to probe the light absorption capacities of pristine ZnO2 and ZnO2/CQD-0.5. Fig. 8 a displayed that no absorption peak in the visible range (380–800 nm) could be observed in the spectrum of ZnO2, suggesting that ZnO2 could not be excited by visible light. After the introduction of CQD, the absorption wavelength was significantly extended to ∼ 500 nm, making it possible to be excited by visible light. Subsequently, the band gap energies of photocatalysts were estimated by Kubelka-Munk function (Eq. (7)) [45]. Where hv and Eg are the photon energy (eV) and band gap energy (eV), respectively. Thus, Eg could be obtained from the extrapolation of Tauc plots which were drawn by (αhv)2 versus photon energy (hv). As shown in Fig. 8b, the band gap energy of ZnO2 was determined to be 3.70 eV, while the band gap energy could be remarkably reduced to 3.18 eV after CQD modification, suggesting that ZnO2/CQD-0.5 could be excited by visible light. Besides, Mott-Schottky test (Fig. 8c) was conducted to clarify the band position of ZnO2. The positive slope indicated that ZnO2 is a n-type semiconductor, and the flat band potential (Efb) was −0.70 V (vs SSCE) obtained from the intercept of the tangent at the x-axis. As for n-type semiconductors, the bottom of conduction band potential (ECB) was approximately equal to the Efb. The potential measured using SSCE (pH = 7) could be converted into the normal hydrogen electrode potential (NHE, pH = 0) via Eq. (8), where Eθ (SSCE) was 0.197 V [54]. Thus, ECB of ZnO2 was calculated to be −0.50 eV (vs NHE), and the valence band potential (EVB) of ZnO2 could be also determined to be 3.20 eV (vs NHE) based on Eq. (9).
(7) αhv = A×(hv − Eg)1/2
(8) E(vsNHE) = E(vsSSCE) + Eθ(SSCE) + 0.059 × pH
(9) ECB = EVB-Eg

Furthermore, PL, EIS and LSV measurements were performed to investigate the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Compared with ZnO2, a significant decrease in the peak intensity could be observed in the PL spectrum of ZnO2/CQD-0.5 (Fig. 8d), indicating a higher separation efficiency of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs after CQD modification [55]. Meanwhile, the EIS Nyquist plot of ZnO2/CQD-0.5 (Fig. 8e) had a smaller arc radius in comparison to pure ZnO2, which suggested that the introduction of CQD could reduce the transfer resistance of electrons in ZnO2, thus facilitating the transfer of the photogenerated electrons. As for the LSV results (Fig. 8f), the photocurrent density in ZnO2/CQD-0.5 was higher than that in ZnO2 under visible-light irradiation. Compared with pure ZnO2, ZnO2/CQD-0.5 showed a significant 3-fold increase in the transient photocurrent response intensity (Fig. S9). These points demonstrated that the addition of CQD could contribute to a higher separation efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, which was in accordance with the above PL results. Considering that ECB and EVB of CQD were 0.57 and 1.87 eV (vs NHE), respectively [56], it could be inferred that the photogenerated electrons in the ZnO2/CQD-0.5 composite transferred from the lower CB of ZnO2 (-0.50 eV) to the higher CB of CQD (0.57 eV), thus effectively accelerating the separation of the photogenerated electron and holes.

The possible generated active species responsible for TC degradation over ZnO2/CQD-0.5 was explored by a series of quenching experiments. According to the previous reports [57,58], 100 mM IPA, 0.5 mM p-BQ, 100 mM FFA, 0.2 mM EDTA and 0.05 mM K2Cr2O7 were used as scavengers for hydroxyl radicals (•OH), superoxide radicals (•O2 −), singlet oxygen (1O2), holes (h+) and electron (e-), respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 9 a, IPA could rarely affect TC degradation, indicating that •OH radicals might not be produced in the photocatalytic system. While, p-BQ and FFA showed an obvious inhibitory effect, and the degradation efficiency at 4 h decreased from 86.1 % to 50 % and 53.2 %, respectively. Accordingly, the rate constant of pseudo-second order kinetics sharply dropped from 1.75 to 0.27 and 0.20 L mg-1h−1 (Fig. 9b). Therefore, •O2 − and 1O2 might be the dominant ROSs generated during the photocatalytic process. It was worth noting that the addition of K2Cr2O7 and EDTA could slightly suppress TC degradation, which implied that the photogenerated e- and h+ participated in the process of TC degradation.

Furthermore, EPR tests were conducted in the ZnO2 and ZnO2/CQD-0.5 photocatalytic systems to verify the generated 1O2 and •O2 −. As shown in Fig. 9c and 9d, there was no signal detected in the dark. Once visible light was introduced for 15 min, the characteristic triplet signal of TEMP-1O2 adduct [59] could be observed in the two systems (Fig. 9c). The generation of 1O2 could be attributed to the photoexcitation of released H2O2 under the catalysis of Zn(OH)2 from the hydrolysis of partial ZnO2 [60]. It should be emphasized that the signal intensity of TEMP-1O2 in the ZnO2/CQD-0.5 spectrum was stronger than that in the ZnO2 spectrum, which might be due to that the released H2O2 was more easily activated to produce 1O2 when visible light was converted to UV light by CQD [61]. However, the characteristic signal of DMPO-•O2 − adduct [62] only appeared in the ZnO2/CQD-0.5 spectrum after visible-light irradiation (Fig. 9d), indicating a crucial contribution of CQD for •O2 − generation. Hence, 1O2 and •O2 − were confirmed to be the main ROSs generated in the ZnO2/CQD-0.5 photocatalytic system. Moreover, CQD modification was more conducive to the production of ROSs, which accounted for the superior visible-light photocatalytic activity of ZnO2/CQD-0.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Process of visible-light excitation for TC degradation and E. Coli inactivation
ce:section.ce:para:
The plausible processes of TC degradation and E. coli inactivation over ZnO2/CQD-0.5 under visible-light irradiation could be deduced and described by Eqs. 10–15. First, CQD could convert visible light into UV light due to its up-conversion property. Then, ZnO2 in the composite could be excited by UV light to form e- and h+ in the CB and VB, respectively. Since the CB of ZnO2 located below the CB of CQD, the produced e- would transfer to the CB of CQD, thus realizing the efficient separation of electron-hole pairs. Subsequently, •O2 − could be produced via the combination of e- and O2, where O2 might come either from the environment or from the decomposition of ZnO2. In this regard, CQD played a dual role in the visible-light excitation of ZnO2/CQD-0.5.
(10)

(11) ZnO2 + UV light → e-(CB)ZnO2 + h+(VB)ZnO2
(12) e-(CB)ZnO2 + CQD → e-(CB)CQD + ZnO2
(13) e-(CB)CQD + O2 → •O2 −+CQD
(14)

(15)

On the other hand, partial ZnO2 in ZnO2/CQD-0.5 could slowly reacted with H2O to produce H2O2 and Zn(OH)2. Herein, the H2O2 release experiment (10 mg of ZnO2/CQD-0.5 in 100 mL of DI water at room temperature) was carried out via a permanganate titration method [59]. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the release rate of H2O2 was less than 10 % even after 4 h of irradiation. Based on the amount of released H2O2, the comparative experiments (H2O2 + visible light, H2O2 + Zn(OH)2 + visible light) were conducted by adding equimolar H2O2 and Zn(OH)2 into TC solution (Fig. 10b). The results indicated that the (H2O2 + visible light) system could hardly degrade TC, while around 50 % of TC was degraded by the (H2O2 + Zn(OH)2 + visible light) system. It meant that H2O2 could be activated to degrade TC by Zn(OH)2 under visible light irradiation. Combined with the above EPR results (Fig. 9c), it could be concluded that the released H2O2 was excited to give 1O2 by visible light in the catalysis of Zn(OH)2 from the hydrolysis of partial ZnO2. What is more, the introduction of CQD tended to convert visible light to UV light, which was more conductive to the activation of H2O2, producing more 1O2 for TC degradation and E. coli inactivation.

On the basis of above analyses and discussion, 1O2 and •O2 − were believed to be the main ROSs for the photocatalytic TC degradation and E. coli inactivation over ZnO2/CQD under visible-light irradiation, and the proposed processes were illustrated in Fig. 11 . In addition to the contribution of reducing the particle size and increasing specific surface area, the CQD in composites played multiple functions in the visible-light excitation of ZnO2: converting visible light to UV light, promoting the separation of electron-hole pairs and enhancing the photoactivation of released H2O2. Thus, ZnO2/CQD showed the excellent visible-light photocatalytic performances towards TC degradation and E. coli inactivation.

In summary, ZnO2/CQD composites were successfully fabricated and used for visible-light photocatalytic TC degradation and E. coli inactivation. The composites exhibited excellent visible-light catalytic activity and high adaptability to pH range, inorganic anion, water resource, oxygen environment and organic pollutant. •O2 − and 1O2 were considered to be the dominant ROSs responsible for TC degradation and E. coli inactivation under visible-light irradiation. A series of characterizations and comparative experiments clearly indicated that the CQD in composites played multiple roles in boosting the visible-light photocatalytic performance of wide-band-gap ZnO2: (1) increasing specific surface area, (2) enhancing visible-light absorption, (3) converting low-energy visible light to high-energy UV light, and (4) accelerating the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs. Owing to the merits of easy preparation, visible-light response, high adaptability and wide application range, it was believed that the self-oxygenated ZnO2/CQD composites would have a bright prospect in the visible-light photocatalytic treatment of organic and bacterial polluted wastewater.
